text:
Admission Date:  1995-08-07              Discharge Date:   2010-12-11

Date of Birth:  2018-11-01             Sex:   F

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
Penicillins / Lactose

Attending:Kayla
Chief Complaint:
Anemia, fatigue


Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None

History of Present Illness:
This is an 85 yo F discharged 6 days ago from ICU after
prolonged hospital stay for c. diff sepsis and hypercapnic
respiratory failure who was trached for inability to wean off of
vent from a likely central process per neuro evaluation.  She
was initally admitted with hypotension, hyponatremia and PNA on
her last admission.

Her hospital course was complicated by:
- multiple failed extubations 10-19 hypercarbia as high as 130's
- UE DVT
- AF with RVR
- s/p trach and PEG
.
Today at her rehab her CXR was noted to have concern of new
infiltrate.  Unclear why a CXR was obtained.
.
In the ED initial VS:  labs remarkable for hct of 20 (down from
24 on discharge).  CXR unchanged from prior.  Sodium 133.  No
other lab abnormalities noted.  UA grossly positive.
.
On evaulation on arrival to the ICU patient reports she is
tired.
.
ROS: Pos per HPI.  Negative for chest pain, shortness of breath,
abd pain, N/V, HA, change in vision, dizziness or light
headedness, fever or chills.

Past Medical History:
Hypercarbic resp failure s/p Trach/PEG
Upper extremity DVT on lovenox
HTN
CVA multiple (most recent 1981) embolic and hemorrhagic
CAD s/p non-Q wave MI (coronary CT did not show significant
Coronary dz)
Atrial fibrillation (not on coumadin due to thalamic CVA in
1981)
Recurrent UTIs (hx of VRE)
H/O urinary stents
Delirium
Depression
Osteoporosis
Glaucoma
L hip fracture
thorax syrinx
h/o sinus pauses (3.1 seconds, patient refused PPM in past)

Social History:
Per recent MICU admission note. Patient has been a resident of
7035 Harris Groves
North George, MN 24482-0399 House Rehab since 05-24. She is divorced. She is
nonambulatory and dependent of most ADLs. She has two children
(one daughter in Alyssa Allen and a son in [**Name2 (NI) **]). She has a remote
smoking history. She smoked at most a few cigarettes per day and
quit in the 1987. No recent history of any alcohol use. Son is
HCP.

Family History:
Non-contributory


Physical Exam:
VS:  97.1 108/47 92 100% on pressure support
GEN: chronically ill appearing F in NAD
HEENT: Trach in place, slightly dry mm, clear OP,
Cards: regular, no murmurs appreciated
Pulm: decreased bs throughout anteriorly, no wheeze or rhonchi
Abd: PEG in place, soft, NTND
Extremities: diffusely anasaric, feet internally rotated
Skin: warm, mild skin breakdown posteriorly
Neuro/Psych: appropriate affect

Pertinent Results:
Microbiology:
05-11: Blood cx x2 pending
12-10: Urine cultur: Preliminary growing pseudomonas,
sensitivities pending

UA: 21-50 WBC/0-2 epi/mod LE/pos nit/mod bacteria
.
Imaging:
CXR:  FINDINGS: Ill-defined bilateral hemidiaphragm, in keeping
with bilateral pleural effusions. There is opacification at the
left lung base, could be atelectasis; however, cannot exclude
pneumonia. Patient has a tracheostomy.  There is no evidence of
pneumothorax.  IMPRESSION: 1. Bilateral pleural effusion. 2.
Opacity at the left lung base, likely atelectasis; cannot
exclude pneumonia.

Brief Hospital Course:
85 year old F recently discharged from the MICU who was
readmitted with anemia (Hct to 20 from 24 on d/c 6 days ago, and
concern for new infiltrate on CXR.

#. CXR FINDINGS:  Repeat CXR was obtainted on admission and was
felt to be unchanged from prior.  She was found to have an
opacity at the left lung base felt to be most likely
atelectasis.  She was not treated for pneumonia during her stay
and her respiratory status remained stable

#. ACUTE ON CHRONIC ANEMIA:  Hct 20 on admission from 24 on
discharge.  Acute drop likely from UTI and chronic anemia from
poor nutritional status and chronic disease.  She remained
hemodynamically stable without evidence of blood loss.  Guaiac
negative. Patient was transfused 1u PRBCs. HCT the following AM
was 25.5 and remained stable.  She should have a repeat
hematocrit checked in 04-24 days.

#. UTI:  Grossly positive UA on admission with no hemodynamic
instability.  She remaiend afebrile, without tachycardia, and
satting well on pressure support.  She also had no leukocytosis
which was reassuring. She was started on Cipro 500mg po bid for
7 days (day 1 = 05-11). Foley catheter was changed.  Her culture
preliminarily grew Pseudomonas, sensitivitites pending at the
time of discharge.

#. HYPERCARBIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE:  S/P trach.  Hypercarbia
likely from central process.  Was evaluated by neurology at last
admission. Patient was continued on pressure support.

Medications on Admission:
Acetaminophen 650 mg prn
Albuterol prn
Alendronate 70 mg qw
Aspirin 81 mg qd
Atorvastatin 40 mg qd
Brimonidine 0.15 % ou Taylor Hospital
Ca/vit D
Enoxaparin 40 mg/0.4 mL Syringe Taylor Hospital
Escitalopram 10 mg qd
Folic Acid 1 mg qd
Furosemide 80 mg qd
Lansoprazole 30 mg qd
Latanoprost 0.005 % Drops ou qhs
Metoprolol Tartrate 12.5 mg Taylor Hospital
Miconazole 2 % Powder
Sennosides 8.6 mg prn
Thiamine 100 mg Tablet
Tolterodine 4 mg Capsule, Sust. Release 24 hr

Discharge Medications:
1. Acetaminophen 325 mg Tablet Taylor Hospital: 1-2 Tablets PO Q6H (every 6
hours) as needed for pain, fever.
2. Albuterol Sulfate 2.5 mg /3 mL (0.083 %) Solution for
Nebulization Taylor Hospital: One (1) neb Inhalation every six (6) hours as
needed for shortness of breath or wheezing.
3. Alendronate 70 mg Tablet Taylor Hospital: One (1) Tablet PO once a week.

4. Aspirin 81 mg Tablet, Chewable Taylor Hospital: One (1) Tablet, Chewable
PO DAILY (Daily).
5. Atorvastatin 40 mg Tablet Taylor Hospital: One (1) Tablet PO HS (at
bedtime).
6. Brimonidine 0.15 % Drops Taylor Hospital: One (1) Drop Ophthalmic Q12H
(every 12 hours).
7. Calcium Carbonate 200 mg (500 mg) Tablet, Chewable Taylor Hospital: One
(1) Tablet, Chewable PO BID (2 times a day).
8. Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) 400 unit Tablet Taylor Hospital: One (1)
Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).
9. Enoxaparin 40 mg/0.4 mL Syringe Taylor Hospital: One (1) syringe
Subcutaneous Q12H (every 12 hours).
10. Escitalopram 10 mg Tablet Taylor Hospital: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
11. Folic Acid 1 mg Tablet Taylor Hospital: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).

12. Furosemide 40 mg Tablet Taylor Hospital: Two (2) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
13. Lansoprazole 30 mg Tablet,Rapid Dissolve, DR Moore: One (1)
Tablet,Rapid Dissolve, DR Moore DAILY (Daily).
14. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg Tablet Moore: 0.5 Tablet PO BID (2
times a day).
15. Miconazole Nitrate 2 % Powder Moore: One (1) Appl Topical QID
(4 times a day) as needed for rash.
16. Heparin, Porcine (PF) 10 unit/mL Syringe Moore: 1-2 MLs
Intravenous PRN (as needed) as needed for line flush.
17. Ciprofloxacin 500 mg Tablet Moore: One (1) Tablet PO Q12H
(every 12 hours) for 5 days: Last day 02-12.
Disp:*10 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
18. Tolterodine 4 mg Capsule, Sust. Release 24 hr [**Month/Day (4) **]: One (1)
Capsule, Sust. Release 24 hr PO once a day.


Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Trinity Medical Ctr East for the Aged - MACU

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary diagnosis:
1. Anemia
2. Complicated urinary tract infection

Secondary diagnosis:
Chronic respiratory failure s/p tracheostomy
Upper extremity DVT


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Bedbound.


Discharge Instructions:
Dear Ms. Bartlett,

It was a pleasure caring for you in the hospital. You were
admitted because of weakness.  Your blood count (hematocrit) was
found to be low. You were transfused 1 unit of blood, and your
blood count came back to your normal level. You were also found
to have a urinary tract infection. We treated you with an
antibiotic called Ciprofloxacin for this. You will need a total
of 7 days of this antibiotic (day 1 = 05-11, last day 02-12).

Other than the Ciprofloxacin, no other changes were made to your
medications.

Followup Instructions:
Please follow up with your doctors at the facility.
